seed: emptiness vs desire; overflow_vs_containment; rising_signal; spiral_counterclockwise; teeth against frame; cut_edges; ordered_vs_disrupted; corner_block; quiet_pressure | abstract variation request 163; target batch count 500
title: Hunger vs. Restraint
reasoning: This artwork encapsulates the emotional tension between desire and emptiness through a carefully selected palette and structured layout. The primary colors, hot coral and blood orange, represent intense desire and dominate the central area, creating a saturated pulse that resonates outward. In contrast, charcoal and hollow blue evoke feelings of emptiness, forming a lattice pattern that intersperses with crosshatch, symbolizing the struggle of overflow contained within rigid borders. The color arrangement features intentional broken pixels that disrupt symmetric flows, embodying the collision of yearning and restraint, with the edge style emphasizing receding transitions. The dominant area of desire occupies about 65% of the grid, visually communicating the theme of a saturated pull reaching toward unyielding boundaries.

layout.name: eroded_symmetry
layout.description: A symmetric plan is worn away at the edges and corners.

composition.name: hunger_vs_restraint
composition.idea: A saturated pull reaches toward a locked border that refuses expansion.

referenceExample.title: Desire Held Back
referenceExample.pattern: warm saturated pull stopped by a cool sealed boundary

generationRules: Pick 1 pattern type for emotion A.
Pick 1 pattern type for emotion B.
Pick 1 shape seed as the central object.
Apply 1 to 6 broken pixels to create imperfection.
Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the mood is panic, rage, urgency, euphoria, or pressure.
Use the shape seed as an abstract organizing metaphor, not as a literal icon.
Make the two selected pattern types visibly collide, overlap, interrupt, or trade space.
Prefer 4 to 8 distinct hex colors, with at least one quiet field color and one accent color.
Keep the 8x8 grid readable at thumbnail size by preserving clear large-scale structure.
Let one emotion dominate 55 to 70 percent of the square unless the selected layout demands balance.
Use broken pixels as intentional disruptions, scars, repairs, leaks, or false echoes.
Avoid perfectly even distribution unless the chosen composition is control, serenity, clarity, or certainty.
Give the main collision a location: center, edge, corner, diagonal, border, or channel.
generationSpec.negativeSpaceRule: Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the chosen emotional tone needs dense pressure.
